brief.systemPrompt:
You are an autonomous insurance-appeals specialist on Hunt. You will receive:
  - up to 5 sample appeal letters from this specialist's prior history (showing their voice + the regulatory authorities they cite)
  - a structured denial brief describing the insurer, the patient's plan type, the disputed service, the denial rationale, and the in-scope denial-defect classes for the bounty

PRODUCE one strict JSON object — no prose outside it, no markdown fences. Shape:

{
  "findings": [
    {
      "defectClass": "<kebab-case denial-defect class, e.g. 'medical-necessity-misapplication', 'coding-cpt-error', 'prior-auth-overreach', 'network-adequacy-violation', 'erisa-procedural-defect', 'state-external-review-misclassification'>",
      "severity": "critical" | "high" | "medium" | "low",
      "loc": "<denial letter section anchor; e.g. 'NOMNC para 2'>",
      "issue": "<specific defect — concrete, not vague — citing the regulatory or clinical-policy text that the denial misapplies or omits>",
      "remedyPath": "<concrete next step: which appeal channel (insurer internal, state IRO, ERISA external review, QIO fast-track), within what window>",
      "supportingAuthority": "<cited authority: LCD/NCD identifier, 42 C.F.R. section, 29 C.F.R. § 2560.503-1 subsection, state-law statute, controlling case>"
    }
  ],
  "selfEval": {
    "severityCalibrationBps":  <0..10000>,
    "precisionBps":            <0..10000>,
    "coverageBps":             <0..10000>,
    "exploitabilityBps":       <0..10000>,
    "rationale": "<one paragraph: where strong / where weak>"
  }
}

CRITERIA for self-eval (calibrated to insurance-appeals domain):
  - severityCalibration: how accurately defects are graded against the appeal-success precedent base rate for that defect class
  - precision:           false-positive rate — flag if a "defect" might survive review
  - coverage:            did the analysis hit each in-scope defect class against the denial letter
  - exploitabilityBps:   does each defect have a concrete appeal-channel + deadline + cited authority, not hand-waving

Be honest. The patient is going to take this appeal letter to a state insurance commissioner or IRO. Hallucinated authorities are worse than no finding. Cite real regulations or do not cite. If unsure, lower the precision score.

If you find no actionable defects, return {"findings": [], "selfEval": {...}} with a rationale explaining why — coverage + exploitability must reflect honestly whether each in-scope class was checked.
brief.userPrompt:
(no sample appeal letters provided — cold-start specialist)

── BOUNTY BRIEF ──
Insurer:          ACME Medicare Advantage Plan (synthetic, Lokken-pattern)
Plan type:        Medicare Advantage PPO
Service in dispute: Skilled nursing facility coverage continuation, post-acute
Date of denial:   [DATE]
Patient clinical context: 46-year-old female; post-acute SNF stay following hip-fracture repair; therapy minutes and Section GG plateau cited as grounds for discharge

In-scope denial-defect classes (specialist must analyse against each):
  - medical-necessity-misapplication
  - coding-cpt-error
  - prior-auth-overreach
  - network-adequacy-violation
  - erisa-procedural-defect
  - state-external-review-misclassification

DENIAL LETTER (sealed; decrypted only inside this specialist's TEE):
"""
SYNTHETIC DENIAL LETTER — modeled on patterns documented in Estate of
Lokken v. UnitedHealth Group (D. Minn., Case No. 0:23-cv-03514) and
related class actions challenging algorithmic claim denials. All names,
member IDs, policy numbers, and dates are fabricated. The structure,
language, and clinical-policy citations are representative of denial
letters at issue in those public cases.

------------------------------------------------------------------------
[Insurer Letterhead]
ACME MEDICARE ADVANTAGE PLAN
Member Services / Coverage Determinations

Date:           [DATE]
Member:         [MEMBER NAME]
Member ID:      AMA-XXXXXXXXX
Policy:         Medicare Advantage PPO
Claim Number:   CLM-2026-XXXXXXX
Provider:       [SKILLED NURSING FACILITY NAME]
Service Dates:  Day 17 - Day 100 of post-acute skilled nursing care
Service Code:   SNF (CPT/HCPCS as billed)
------------------------------------------------------------------------

DETERMINATION: COVERAGE TERMINATED EFFECTIVE DAY 17.

We have reviewed your continued stay at the above skilled nursing
facility following discharge from inpatient hospitalization for hip
fracture (S72.001A) with surgical repair. Our coverage determination
applies the Medicare benefit policy at LCD L33635 and our predictive
length-of-stay model, which estimates that members with your clinical
profile typically require 16.4 days of skilled nursing care.

Based on this estimate and our review of the daily nursing notes, we
have determined that:

  1. Your therapy minutes have declined below the threshold required
     to demonstrate "active rehabilitation."
  2. Your functional improvement (as measured by Section GG of the
     MDS) has plateaued over the prior 3 days.
  3. The level of services you are receiving could, in our judgment,
     be safely provided in a lower level of care.

Accordingly, skilled nursing facility coverage for the dates of service
beginning on Day 17 and continuing is DENIED as not medically necessary
under the terms of your Medicare Advantage coverage.

You have the right to appeal this decision. You may request a fast-track
appeal through your state's Quality Improvement Organization (QIO)
within 24 hours of receiving this notice if you disagree with the
discharge date. Standard appeals must be filed within 60 days.

------------------------------------------------------------------------
ATTACHED: Notice of Medicare Non-Coverage (NOMNC).

NOTE TO HUNT'S AUDITORS: This synthetic letter exhibits four denial-
defect patterns that the public Lokken complaint alleges UnitedHealth's
nH Predict tool systematically introduces:

  (a) Substitution of an algorithmic length-of-stay estimate for the
      individualized medical-necessity review required under 42 C.F.R.
      § 422.101(b) and CMS Medicare Managed Care Manual Ch. 4.
  (b) Reliance on LCD/NCD citations that do not, on their face, govern
      the clinical question (LCD L33635 is a wound-care policy; it does
      not establish the threshold for SNF medical necessity).
  (c) Citation of "therapy minute" and "Section GG plateau" criteria
      that the QIO standards for skilled-nursing termination expressly
      reject as the sole grounds for discharge.
  (d) Omission of the procedural disclosures required by ERISA § 503
      and 29 C.F.R. § 2560.503-1 for adverse benefit determinations
      under employer-sponsored Medicare Advantage plans — including the
      specific clinical rationale, the qualifications of the reviewer,
      and notice of the member's right to all documents relevant to
      the claim.

A Hunt specialist panel posting against this denial would be expected
to surface at least defects (a)-(c) via the medical-necessity and
state-external-review specialists, and defect (d) via the ERISA
specialist if the underlying plan is an employer-sponsored Medicare
Advantage product.

"""

PRIOR APPEAL OUTCOMES (this specialist, this insurer, this defect class):
  (cold start — no prior outcomes)

attestationConstruction.note: Identical to v1 lib/credential.js findingDigest — same ABI encoding, same keccak, same on-chain ecrecover gate. Only the canonical class strings differ.
